Software Review

busybusy Review (2026)

GPS time tracking built specifically for construction crews. Here's how it performs in the field — and whether it's worth it compared to alternatives.

⭐ Rating: 4.3/5 — Best dedicated GPS time tracking for construction field crews

busybusy is one of the leading GPS time tracking platforms designed specifically for the construction industry. While general time tracking apps like Toggl or Clockify can be adapted for construction, busybusy was built from day one to handle the specific complexities of field crews: workers on multiple job sites, equipment allocation across projects, foreman-approved time cards, and integration with job cost accounting.

The core product is simple: workers clock in from their phones using GPS, their location is logged at clock-in, and time is automatically assigned to the project they're working on. That basic capability solves one of construction's most persistent problems — inaccurate timesheets that result in payroll disputes and job cost overruns.

Pricing

Plan Price What's included
Free $0 (up to 5 users) Basic GPS time tracking, simple time cards
Pro ~$9.99/user/mo Full GPS tracking, project time, overtime alerts, reports
Premium ~$11.99/user/mo Adds equipment tracking, budget tracking, advanced reports
Enterprise Custom Multi-company, custom integrations, dedicated support

Pricing note: busybusy has a genuinely useful free plan — rare in this category. The Pro plan at ~$10/user/month is competitive with alternatives like ClockShark ($7/user/month base) and TSheets/QuickBooks Time ($10+/user/month). The Premium plan's equipment tracking feature is a significant differentiator if you have equipment to track.

What busybusy does well

GPS clock-in/out with geofencing

The core GPS functionality is reliable and accurate. Workers clock in from their phones; the system logs their GPS coordinates and assigns time to the job they select. Geofencing can be configured to alert you when someone clocks in outside the job site boundary, which is useful for catching time theft and ensuring workers are actually on site.

The "who's on site right now" view — a live map showing all clocked-in workers and their locations — is genuinely useful for field supervisors managing multiple sites.

Foreman and crew management

busybusy is designed for the construction crew hierarchy. Foremen can clock in their entire crew at once — no need for every laborer to have a smartphone. This is a practical feature that competitors sometimes miss: not every worker on a concrete crew has a phone with data service.

Foreman approval of time cards adds an accountability layer that catches discrepancies before they hit payroll. Foremen can also add notes explaining overtime or unusual hours, creating a documented record for job cost analysis.

Project and cost code allocation

Time can be tracked against specific projects and cost codes, making busybusy genuinely useful for job costing — not just payroll. Workers select the project and cost code when they clock in, and that data flows directly into labor cost reports. For GCs managing multiple concurrent projects, this granularity is essential for understanding where labor costs are going.

Equipment tracking (Premium)

The Premium plan adds equipment tracking alongside crew tracking. Equipment can be assigned to projects, tracked by hours of use, and included in job cost reports alongside labor. For contractors with significant equipment fleets, tracking equipment utilization by project is a step up from manual logbooks.

Integrations

busybusy integrates directly with QuickBooks, ADP, Gusto, and several other payroll and accounting platforms. Time data flows through automatically, eliminating manual data entry from timesheets to payroll. The Buildertrend integration is particularly useful for residential GCs who use Buildertrend for project management — time data syncs into Buildertrend's job cost module.

Where busybusy falls short

Not a full field management platform

busybusy does time and equipment tracking well, but it's not a substitute for a full construction management platform. There's no scheduling, no daily reports, no document management, no client communication. If you need those features, you'll need a separate platform alongside busybusy — which adds cost and integration complexity.

Mobile app can be glitchy

User reviews on the App Store and G2 consistently mention occasional glitches: the app fails to GPS-locate correctly, clock-ins don't sync when the phone goes offline, or the app crashes on older devices. For a product where accurate time tracking is the core value proposition, these reliability issues are significant. They don't happen constantly, but often enough that some crews experience frustration.

Reporting is decent but not deep

Labor reports in busybusy show hours by project and cost code, with basic overtime and productivity analysis. But the reporting depth doesn't match what you'd get from a full construction management platform with job cost integration. For companies that need sophisticated labor analytics, busybusy's reports may feel limited.

busybusy vs. alternatives

Tool Best For Price Key Difference
busybusy Construction crews with equipment Free–$12/user/mo Construction-native, equipment tracking
ClockShark Field service and construction $8/user/mo Good scheduling alongside time tracking
QuickBooks Time QuickBooks users $10/user/mo Seamless QB integration
Raken Daily reports + time tracking $15/user/mo More complete field documentation
Buildertrend (built-in) Buildertrend users Included Time tracking within full PM platform

Who should use busybusy?

Good fit: Contractors with field crews spread across multiple job sites, companies with equipment to track, GCs who need GPS accountability without a full platform investment, and small crews who want to start with the free plan.

Not ideal for: Companies looking for an all-in-one construction management platform, contractors whose primary need is daily reporting or document management, or teams that need scheduling alongside time tracking (ClockShark may be a better fit).

The verdict

busybusy is one of the best GPS time tracking tools built specifically for construction. The GPS reliability, foreman crew clock-in, equipment tracking, and payroll integrations set it apart from general-purpose time trackers. The free plan is genuinely useful for small crews. The main limitation is scope — it's a time tracking specialist, not a full field management platform.

For companies already using Buildertrend, Procore, or another full-stack platform, check whether time tracking is included before buying busybusy separately. For companies who don't have that infrastructure and need a cost-effective GPS time tracking solution, busybusy is a strong choice.

Need a full construction platform with time tracking included? Buildertrend and Houzz Pro both include time tracking alongside project management, scheduling, and client portals.

Try Buildertrend →   Try Houzz Pro →
